subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: Which of the following method of applying water may be used on rolling land?
solution: Sol. (d). Irrigation is an artificial application of water in accordance with the water requirement of crops throughout the growth period to attain full maturity. We have different techniques of Irrigation. Some of them are described below. 1. Free Flooding: Also called as Ordinary Flooding. This method is also called as Wild flooding because flow of water is not controlled in this method. This is preferred when large quantity of water is available and field is irregular. This technique is suitable for sloppy land and crops like pastures. 2. Border Flooding: In this method agricultural field is divided into number of strips of land by construction of levees which serve the purpose of border. 3. Check Flooding: It is a modified form of ordinary flooding in which water is controlled by construction of low and flat levees. This method is suitable for high and low permeable soil. 4. Furrow Flooding: Furrows are narrow ditches which are excavated between rows of crops and carry irrigation water through it. It is preferred in the crops which are grown in rows, for example Sugarcane. In this method only 20-50% of agricultural field is wetted. Hence evaporation losses are reduced. Hence option (d).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: If the electrical conductivity of water is in between 250 to 750 $\mu$mho/cm at 25°C then it is classified as?
solution: Sol. (b). Electrical conductivity is the measure of the amount of electrical current a material can carry or its ability to carry a current. The concentration of soluble salt in irrigation water can be expressed as Electrical conductivity measured in $\mu$mho/cm. Hence option (b).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: Sodium absorption ratio (SAR) is defined as?
solution: Sol. (c). The concentration of Sodium ions is expressed in the form of Sodium Absorption Ratio (S.A.R.). The below formula represents the S.A.R. $\text{S.A.R.} = \frac{[Na^+]}{\sqrt{\frac{[(Ca^{+2})+(Mg^{+2})]}{2}}}$ Where these concentrations are represented in milli equivalent per litre of Sodium, Calcium and Magnesium respectively. Hence option (c).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: The value of Sodium Absorption Ratio for high sodium water lies between?
solution: Sol. (c). The concentration of Sodium ions is expressed in the form of Sodium Absorption Ratio (S.A.R.). The below formula represents the S.A.R. $\text{S.A.R.} = \frac{[Na^+]}{\sqrt{\frac{[(Ca^{+2})+(Mg^{+2})]}{2}}}$ Where these concentrations are represented in milli equivalent per litre of Sodium, Calcium and Magnesium respectively. Hence option (c).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: Optimum depth of kor watering for rice crop is?
solution: Sol. (c). Kor watering: It is observed that water requirement of a crop is maximum when it is few centimetres high. This watering applied to the crop when it is few centimetres high within a certain fixed interval is called as Kor watering. For rice, optimum depth of kor watering is 190mm as derived from practical result. This depth also corresponds to kor depth which is depth of water applied during kor watering period. Hence option (c).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: Irrigation water having the concentration of $Na^{++}$, $Ca^{++}$, $Mg^{++}$, and as 20, 3 and 1 milli-equivalent per litre respectively will be classified as?
solution: Sol. (b). The concentration of Sodium ions is expressed in the form of Sodium Absorption Ratio (S.A.R.). The below formula represents the S.A.R. $\text{S.A.R.} = \frac{[Na^+]}{\sqrt{\frac{[(Ca^{+2})+(Mg^{+2})]}{2}}}$ Where these concentrations are represented in milli equivalent per liter of Sodium, Calcium and Magnesium respectively. Putting the above values of these ions in S.A.R. formula, we have $\text{S.A.R.} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{\frac{3.0+1.0}{2}}} = 14.142$. When S.A.R. values lies between 10 - 18, then that type of irrigation water is called as Medium Sodium water. Hence option (b).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: The relation between duty D in hectares/cumec, depth of water $\Delta$ in meters and base period B in days is given by?
solution: Sol. (b). The relationship between D, B and $\Delta$ is given by below formula. $\Delta = \frac{8.64B}{D}$ Where '$\Delta$' represents the 'Delta of a Crop' which is defined as the total summation of water depth applied to the crop throughout the crop period. Its unit can be cm or m. 'B' represents the Base period of a crop in days, which is defined as the time interval from the instant of first watering to the instant of last watering. Where 'D' represents the Duty of a crop, which is defined as the total number of hectares of land irrigated when 1 $m^3$/s of discharge is supplied continuously throughout the base period of a crop. Its unit is hectare/cumec. Hence option (b).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: The duty is largest?

options.a: At the head of water course
options.c: At the head of a main canal

solution: Sol. (b). Duty of a crop, which is defined as the total number of hectares of land irrigated when 1 $m^3$/s of discharge is supplied continuously throughout the base period of a crop. Its unit is hectare/cumec. $\text{Duty} = \frac{\text{Area of land irrigated (in hectare)}}{\text{Discharge encountered (}\frac{m^3}{s}\text{)}}$ During the movement of water from the head of the main canal to the field, water is lost in form of seepage and absorption loss. That why Duty at the head of the field is more than the head of the canal. Therefore, as we move from upstream to downstream in a canal irrigation network at various points of measurement Duty is increased. Hence Duty will maximum 'on the field' out of all four option. Hence option (b).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: The "outlet discharge factor" is the duty at the head of?
solution: Sol. (c). The outlet of minor that is inlet of the water course is called as Outlet. Outlet represents the boundary between the state irrigation department and farmers. Duty at the outlet is called as 'Outlet discharge factor'. Hence option (c).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: The kor depth for rice is 190mm and kor period is 14 days. the outlet factor for this will be?
solution: Sol. (a). Outlet Factor, which is defined as the total number of hectares of land irrigated when 1 $m^3$/s of discharge is supplied continuously throughout the base period of a crop. Its unit is hectare/cumec. Also called as Duty at outlet. $\Delta = \frac{8.64B}{D}$ $\Delta$=the depth of a crop. (in m) B=the base period (in days) D=the duty of a crop. (in hectare/cumec) Therefore, putting the value, we get $0.190 = \frac{8.64 \times 14}{D} \Rightarrow D = \frac{8.64 \times 14}{0.190} = 636.631 \equiv 637 \text{ ha/cumec}$. Hence option (a).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: For supplying water to rabi crop, kharif crop and sugarcane, the channel is designed for a capacity equal to the greater of the water requirement of?

options.c: rabi and sugarcane or kharif and Sugarcane

solution: Sol. (c). Rabi crops or rabi harvest are agricultural crops that are sown in winter and harvested in the spring. Some of the rabi crop wheat and peas. Kharif crops, monsoon crops or autumn crops are domesticated plants like rice that are cultivated and harvested in India during the Indian subcontinent's monsoon season, which lasts from June to November depending on the area. Rice is the most important kharif crop of India. Perennial crops are crops which are alive year-round and are harvested multiple times before dying. Example is sugarcane. Hence $Q_{\text{design}} = \max \{Q_r + Q_s, Q_k + Q_s\}$ Where $Q_r$ = Discharge required during rabi season. $Q_s$ = discharge required for sugarcane crop. $Q_k$ = discharge required during kharif season. Hence option (c).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: The ratio of the quantity of water stored in the root zone of the crop to the quantity of water actually delivered in the fields is known as?
solution: Sol. (b). During the passage of water from the head of the main canal to the field water is lost in various forms therefore following irrigation efficiency has to be defined. (ii) Water Application Efficiency = $\frac{\text{Volume of waters getting stored in the root zone}}{\text{Volume of water actually supplied to the field}}$. Hence option (b).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: The water utilizable by plants is available in soils mainly in the form of?
solution: Sol. (b). Field Capacity: The moisture contains in the root zone when all the gravity water has just infiltrated. Gravity water: The water that drains off easily by gravity. Capillary water: It is that component of field capacity which is held by the soil particles with molecular attraction and is available for the roots to be extracted under the effect of capillarity. Also called as available water. Hydroscopic Water: The small amount of water that forms an extremely thin, tightly held film around the soil particles is termed as hydroscopic water. It is also unavailable for the plants. Chemical Water: Water that contains chemical which may get polluted due to some reason. Hence option (b).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: The amount of irrigation water required to meet the evapotranspiration needs of the crop during its full growth is called?
solution: Sol. (c). Consumptive irrigation requirement (C.I.R) - The amount of irrigation water required in order to meet the evapotranspiration requirement of the crop during its full growth. It is therefore the consumptive use but exclusive of effective precipitation stored in the soil moisture or ground water. $\text{C.I.R} = C_u - R_e$ Where, $R_e$ = effective rainfall. $C_u$ = Consumptive use.

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: With the increase in the quantity of water supplied, the yield of most crops?

options.c: increases up to a certain limit and then becomes constant
options.d: increases up to a certain limit and then decreases

solution: Sol. (d). The quantity of water increasing up to a field capacity (FC) the yielding of crop increases. Field Capacity: The moisture contains in the root zone when all the gravity water has just infiltrated. When the water content at which soil is fully saturated that is at saturation capacity the yielding of crops decreases. Because for healthy growth of plants, soil must have good water holding capacity and air holding capacity. Hence option (d).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Hydrograph is the graphical representation of?
solution: Sol. (a). Hydrograph is the graph drawn between flood discharge which include both direct runoff and base flow against time duration to represent stream flow variation for a rainfall. The draining away of water from the surface of an area of land, a building or structure, etc. is called as Runoff. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Infiltration rate is always?

options.c: equal to or less than the infiltration capacity
options.d: equal to or more than the infiltration capacity

solution: Sol. (c). Infiltration is the process by which water on the ground surface enters the soil. Infiltration rate is a measure of the rate at which a particular soil is able to absorb rainfall or irrigation water. Infiltration rate is always equal to or less than the infiltration capacity because it is the maximum rate of infiltration performed by specific type of soil under any condition depending upon intensity of rainfall. Hence option (c).

subject: Irrigation Engineering
question_text: The depth of water required to bring the soil moisture content of a given soil up-to its field capacity is called?
solution: Sol. (c). Soil moisture deficiency is defined as the depth of water required to bring the soil moisture content of a given soil up to its field capacity. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Infiltration capacity?

options.d: changes with both time and location

solution: Sol. (d). Infiltration is the process by which water on the ground surface enters the soil. Infiltration capacity changes with both time and location which has been considered in Horton's Infiltration Equation. Hence option (d).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Infiltration is the?

options.a: movement of water through the soil
options.b: absorption of water by soil surface

solution: Sol. (a). Infiltration is the process by which water on the ground surface enters the soil. Infiltration is the movement of water through the soil. It occurs at interface of soil surface and surrounding air. The movement of water within the soil mass is called as percolation. The difference between infiltration and percolation is that infiltration occurs at soil surface but movement of water takes place in both the cases. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: If the intensity of rainfall is more than the infiltration capacity of soil, then the infiltration rate will be?
solution: Sol. (b). $i > f_c \Rightarrow f = f_c$ where, $i$ = Intensity of rainfall, $f_c$ = Infiltration capacity, $f$ = actual rate of infiltration. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Cyclonic precipitation is caused by lifting of an air mass due to?
solution: Sol. (a). Orographic precipitation is the result from lifting of warm moisture laden air masses due to topographic barriers. Convective precipitation occurs when air rises vertically through the self-sustaining mechanism of convection. Cyclonic precipitation is caused by the lifting of an air mass because of the pressure difference. If low pressure occurs at an area, air will flow horizontally from the surrounding area, causing the air at the low-pressure area to lift. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Which of the following is a non-recording rain gauge?
solution: Sol. (b). Non-recording rain gauge: Symon's rain gauge. Recording rain gauge: 1. Tipping bucket type, 2. Weighing bucket type, 3. Floating type (Siphon mechanism).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: A rain gauge should preferably be fixed?
solution: Sol. (c). A rain gauge should preferable be fixed in an open space. A Rainguage must be surrounded by an open fenced area of at least 5.5m x 5.5m. No object should be nearer to the instrument than 30.0 m or twice the height of obstruction, whichever is more. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: If allowable percentage error in the estimate of basic rainfall is E and coefficient of variation of rainfall is $C_v$, then the optimum number of rain gauge is given by?
solution: Sol. (c). Optimum no. of rain gauge(N) = $(\frac{C_v}{E})^2$. $C_v$ = Coefficient of variation of rainfall expressed in percentage for the existing set of rainguage station. E = permissible or allowable percentage of error in estimation of mean rainfall.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Which of the following types of rain gauge is used for measuring rain in remote hilly inaccessible areas?
solution: Sol. (a). Tipping Bucket Type- This is 30.5 cm size Rainguage having a pan of small bucket. It is generally used in a U.S.A. The bucket is so balanced that when 0.25 mm is collected on the bucket it tips and bring another bucket in the position and water is stored in a storage vessel. The movement of the tipping bucket can be transmitted electronically over distances. So, tipping bucket can be installed in the hilly or inaccessible areas, from where they can supply measurements directly in the control room.

subject: Hydrology
question_text:
Rate of evaporation from a water surface increases if
(i) difference of vapour pressure between water and air is increased
(ii) velocity of wind is decreased
(iii) concentration of soluble solids in water is decreased
the correct answer is?
solution:
1. Rate of evaporation (R.O.E) directly proportional to difference of vapour pressure between water and air.
2. (R.O.E) $\propto \frac{1}{\text{atmospheric pressure}}$
3. (R.O.E) $\propto$ Temperature
4. (R.O.E) $\propto$ Velocity of wind
5. (R.O.E) $\propto \frac{1}{\text{Concentration of soluble solids in water}}$
6. (R.O.E) $\propto$ Exposed surface area
7. (R.O.E) $\propto \frac{1}{\text{Humidity}}$ i.e. if the humidity is more than evaporation will be less.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: A 70% index of wetness means.
solution:
Index of Wetness = $\frac{\text{rainfall in a given year at a given place}}{\text{average annual rainfall of that place}} \times 100$
Average Annual Rainfall of that place is calculated using past 30 years data.
The deficit in the Rainfall from the average is called as Rain deficiency. % Rain deficiency = $100 - \% \text{ index of Wetness}$.
Hence 70% of index of wetness is equal to 30% of Rain Deficiency.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Under the same conditions, which of the following shapes of water surface will give the highest rate of evaporation?

options.d: independent of shapes of water surface.

solution: The rate of evaporation is directly proportional to the velocity of the air flow. Convex increase the velocity of air more than concave. Hence convex has more evaporation rate than concave water surface.

subject: Hydrology
question_text:
Assertion A: To estimate the rainfall over a catchment, the number of rain gauge required per unit area is large for hilly areas.
Reason R: Rainfall gradient is steep.
Select your correct answer according to the coding system given below:

options.a: both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.b: both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.c: A is true but R is false
options.d: A is false but R is true

solution: Due to Rainfall gradient is steep, the number of rain gauge required per unit area is large for hilly areas. As per the IS Recommendation for Hilly Areas of heavy rainfall, 1 Rainguage covers an area of 130 $\text{km}^2$. Whereas for plain areas 1 Rainguage covers an area of 520 $\text{km}^2$.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: When surface of transpiration is submerged under water, then potential evapotranspiration is?

options.d: equal to or less than evapotranspiration.

solution:
When surface of transpiration is submerged under water, then potential evapotranspiration is much more than evapotranspiration. Because Potential evaporation or potential evapotranspiration is defined as the amount of evaporation that would occur if a sufficient water source were available.
Evapo-Transpiration - is the sum of the water lost to the atmosphere by the plant through transpiration and water evaporated from the soil or water body surrounding the plants.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Coefficient of variation is given by?
solution:
$C_v = \text{Coefficient of variation of rainfall expressed in percentage for the existing set of rainguage station.}$
$C_v = \frac{\sigma}{P_{avg}} \times 100$
Where $\sigma = \text{Standard deviation}$
Calculated using below formula.
$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{n}{n-1} (P^2_{avg} - {P_{avg}}^2)}$
where n = no of existing rainguage.
$P_{avg} = (P_1 + P_2 + P_3 + \dots P_n) / n$
$P^2_{avg} = ({P_1}^2 + {P_2}^2 + {P_3}^2 + \dots {P_n}^2) / n$

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Unit of runoff in M.K.S. system is
solution:
The draining away of water from the surface of an area of land, a building or structure, etc. is called as Runoff. Unit of runoff in M.K.S system is cubic meter/sec.
There are three major types of runoff depending upon the source- Surface flow, interflow, base flow.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The runoff increases with?
solution:
The draining away of water from the surface of an area of land, a building or structure, etc. is called as Runoff.
1. The runoff increases with increase in intensity of rain.
2. The runoff decreases with increase in infiltration capacity. Infiltration is the process by which water on the ground surface enters the soil. It is commonly used in both hydrology and soil sciences. The infiltration capacity is defined as the maximum rate of infiltration.
3. The runoff decreases with increase in permeability of soil. Soil permeability is the property of the soil to transmit water and air.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The area between the isohyets 45 cm and 55 cm is 100 square km and between 55 cm and 65 cm is 150 square km. The average depth of annual precipitation over the above basin of 250 square km will be?
solution:
Given Area ($A_1$) = 100 $km^2$ between the isohyets 45cm and 55cm.
Area ($A_2$) = 150 $km^2$ between the isohyets 55cm and 65cm.
$P_{avg} = \frac{(\frac{P_1+P_2}{2})A_1 + (\frac{P_2+P_3}{2})A_2 + \dots + (\frac{P_{n-1}+P_n}{2})A_{n-1}}{A_1+A_2+\dots+A_{n-1}}$
$P_{avg} = \frac{(\frac{45+55}{2})100 + (\frac{55+65}{2})150}{100+150} = \frac{14000}{250} = 56.0\text{ cm}$

subject: Hydrology
question_text: A current meter is used to measure the?
solution: The velocity of flow of water at any point in the open channel can be most accurately and conveniently determined by means of a mechanical device named Current meter.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: If it rains between 2 P.M. and 3 P.M. and the entire basin area just starts contributing water at 3 P.M. to the outlet, then time of concentration will be?
solution:
The shortest duration of rainfall which is similar to the time taken by the rainwater droplet to travel from farthest area towards outlet is called time of concentration ($t_c$).
Here, $t_c$ is the time difference between 2 pm and 3pm.
$t_c = 1\text{ hour} = 60\text{ minutes}$.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The rainfall on five successive days were measured as 100 mm, 80 mm, 60 mm, 40 mm, and 20 mm respectively. If the infiltration index or the storm loss rate for the catchment area is earlier estimated as 50 mm/day, the total surface run off will be?
solution:
Given, the rainfall on five successive days were measured as 100mm, 80mm, 60mm, 40mm, and 20mm respectively.
Infiltration index or the storm loss rate = 50mm/day.
Here, Rainfall = Runoff + (Infiltration index) $\times$ (time period)
The period in which the rainfall intensity is greater than infiltration index that period of storm will cause runoff.
Hence $4^{\text{th}}$ and $5^{\text{th}}$ day will not contribute towards runoff.
Therefore
$100 + 80 + 60 = \text{Runoff} + 50 \times 3$
Surface Runoff = 240 - 150 = 90mm

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The normal annual precipitation at stations X, A, B and C are 700 mm, 1000 mm, 900 mm and 800 mm respectively. If the storm precipitation at three station A, B and C were 100 mm, 90mm, and 80 mm respectively, then the storm precipitation for station X will be?
solution:
Given normal annual precipitation $N_x=700\text{mm}$, $N_A=1000\text{mm}$, $N_B=900\text{mm}$, $N_C=800\text{mm}$
Precipitation at three station
$P_A = 100\text{ mm}$
$P_B = 90\text{ mm}$
$P_C = 80\text{ mm}$
If normal precipitation at any of these selected stations differ by more than 10% of selected station than we use normal ratio method.
$P_x = \frac{N_x}{M} [\frac{P_1}{N_1} + \frac{P_2}{N_2} + \frac{P_3}{N_3} + \dots \frac{P_m}{N_m}]$
Check: - $\frac{N_a - N_x}{N_x} \times 100 = \frac{1000 - 700}{700} \times 100 = 42.857 > 10\%$
Means normal ratio method can be used.
$P_x = \frac{700}{3} [\frac{100}{1000} + \frac{90}{900} + \frac{80}{800}] = 70\text{mm}$

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The best unit duration of storm for a unit hydrograph is?
solution:
A hydrograph may be used to show how the water flow in a drainage basin (particularly river runoff) responds to a period of rain.
This type of hydrograph is known as a storm or flood hydrograph and it is generally drawn with two vertical axes. One is used to plot a line graph showing the discharge of a river in cumec (cubic meters per second) at a given point over a period of time.
The second is used to plot a bar graph of the rainfall event which precedes the changes in discharge.
The scale on the horizontal axis is usually in hours or days and this allows both the rain event to be recorded and the subsequent changes in river discharge to be plotted.
The shape of the hydrograph varies according to a number of controlling factors in the drainage basin but it will generally include the following features.
The baseflow of the river represents the normal day to day discharge of the river and is the consequence of groundwater seeping into the river channel. The rising limb of the hydrograph represents the rapid increase in resulting from rainfall causing surface runoff and then later throughflow. Peak discharge occurs when the river reaches its highest level. The time difference between the peak of the rain event and the peak discharge is known as the lag time or basin lag. The falling limb (or recession limb as it is sometimes known) is when discharge decreases and the river's level fall. It has a gentler gradient than the rising limb as most overland flow has now been discharged and it is mainly throughflow which is making up the river water.
For a unit hydrograph, the best unit duration of storm is one fourth of basin lag.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The unit hydrograph due to a storm may be obtained by dividing the ordinates of the direct runoff hydrograph by?
solution:
The ordinate of the Unit Hydrograph = $\frac{\text{Ordinate of Direct Runoff Hydrograph}}{\text{Direct Runoff in cm}}$.
Hence to obtain UH from DRH you have to divide the ordinate by the direct runoff depth in cm.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The unit hydrograph of a specified duration can be used to evaluate the hydrograph of storm of ?
solution:
Unit hydrograph is a direct runoff hydrograph resulting from one unit (one inch or one cm) of constant intensity uniform rainfall occurring over the entire watershed. The concept of unit hydrograph is based on linear systems theory and follow the principles of superposition and proportionality.
The hydrograph of storms of any duration can be determined by using the unit hydrograph of a specified duration.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: S-hydrograph is used to obtain unit hydrograph of?

options.a: shorter duration from long longer duration

solution:
The method of simple superposition is applicable only when other duration is integral multiple of the available duration of the unit hydrograph. However, many at times it becomes necessary to generate a unit hydrograph of other duration which is not integral multiple of the available duration. In such cases it becomes necessary to evolve a general method based on the principle of superposition. Such a method has been evolved by hydrologists which is called 'S' hydrograph method.
A 'S' hydrograph is nothing but a hydrograph generated by a continuous effective rainfall occurring at a uniform rate for an indefinite period. It is called 'S' hydrograph because the shape of the hydrograph comes out like alphabet 'S' though slightly deformed.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: If two 4-hour unit hydrograph are staggered by 4 hours and added graphically, the resulting hydrograph will be?
solution: Unit hydrograph is a direct runoff hydrograph resulting from one unit (one inch or one cm) of constant intensity uniform rainfall occurring over the entire watershed. The concept of unit hydrograph is based on linear systems theory and follow the principles of superposition and proportionality. Using the Sherman theory of Unit Hydrograph with assumption of Linear response, if two 4-hour unit hydrograph are staggered by 4-hour and added graphically the resulting hydrograph will be 8-hour direct runoff hydrograph (DRH) with 20 cm (or mm) runoff depth.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: According to Dicken's formula for estimating floods, the peak discharge is proportional to?
solution:
Dicken's formula
$Q_p = C_D A^{3/4}$
Where $C_D = \text{Constant}$
A = catchment area in $\text{km}^2$.
It was formerly adopted only in Northern India but now it can be used in most of the States in India after proper modification of the constant. According to the area of catchment and amount of rainfall, C varies from 11.37 to 22.04.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The relation between probability (P) and recurrence interval (T) is given by?
solution:
Probability(P) = $\frac{1}{T}$
Where probability(P) defines the possibility of occurrence of flood whose return period is T.
'T' represents the Recurrence interval or return period of a flood. A return period, also known as a recurrence interval or repeat interval, is an average time or an estimated average time between events such as earthquakes, floods, landslides, or a river discharge flows to occur.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Dimensions of coefficient of transmissibility are?
solution:
Coefficient of transmissibility(T): It is the rate of flow of water through a vertical strip aquifer of unit width (1 meter).
Transmissibility = kB
Where k = coefficient of permeability whose unit is cm/s with dimension as $M^0 L^1 T^{-1}$. The coefficient of permeability of a soil describes how easily a water will move through a soil. It is also commonly referred to as the hydraulic conductivity of a soil. This factor can be affected by the viscosity, or thickness(fluidity) of a water and its density.
and B = width of an aquifer in meter whose dimension is $M^0 L^1 T^0$.
Hence that of Transmissibility = $M^0 L^1 T^{-1} \times M^0 L^1 T^0 = M^0 L^2 T^{-1}$.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: If d is the depth of the aquifer through which water is flowing, then the relationship between permeability K and transmissibility T is given by?
solution:
Coefficient of transmissibility(T): It is the rate of flow of water through a vertical strip aquifer of unit width (1 meter).
Transmissibility = kd
Where k = coefficient of permeability whose unit is cm/s. The coefficient of permeability of a soil describes how easily a water will move through a soil. It is also commonly referred to as the hydraulic conductivity of a soil. This factor can be affected by the viscosity, or thickness(fluidity) of a water and its density.
d = depth of an aquifer in meter.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: An artesian aquifer is the one where?

options.a: water surface under the ground is at atmospheric pressure
options.b: water is under pressure between two impervious strata
options.c: water table serves as upper surface of zone of saturation

solution:
An artesian aquifer is a confined aquifer containing groundwater under positive pressure. An artesian aquifer has trapped water, surrounded by layers of impermeable rock or clay which apply positive pressure to the water contained within the aquifer.
An artesian aquifer is the aquifer where water is under pressure between two impervious strata. It is also known as confined aquifer where aquifer being a geological formation.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: A deep well?

options.a: is always deeper than a shallow well
options.b: has more discharge than a shallow well
options.c: is weaker structurally than a shallow well

solution:
A well is an excavation or structure created in the ground by digging, driving, or drilling to access liquid resources, usually water.
An open well is classified as
- Shallow Well
- Deep Well.
The nomenclature of shallow and deep well has nothing to do with the actual depth of the well. A deep well is a well which is supported on a mota layer and draws its water supply through a hole bored in it from the previous foundation below the mota layer. A shallow well on the other hand, penetrates the pervious stratum only and draws its water supply through it. The term mota layer also sometimes known as matbarwa or nagasan, refers to a layer of clay, cemented sand, kankar or any other hard material. The mota layer gives structural support to the open well.
Actually, shallow well can be deeper than deep well. However, since a shallow well draws water from the first pervious stratum, the water is liable to be contaminated by rain water percolating in the vicinity, and may take with its mineral organic matter such as decomposing animals and plants. The water in a deep well is not liable to get such impurities and infection. Also, the pervious formation below a mota layer normally has greater water content and specific yield. Hence discharge from a deep well is generally more than shallow well.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: A multipurpose reservoir is the one which is?

options.a: designed for one purpose but serves more than one purpose
options.b: planned and constructed to serves various purposes

solution:
A Reservoir is most commonly, an enlarged natural or artificial lake, pond or impoundment created using a dam or lock to store water.
Reservoirs can be created in a number of ways, including controlling a watercourse that drains an existing body of water, interrupting a watercourse to form an embayment within it, through excavation, or building any number of retaining walls or levees.
A multipurpose reservoir is the one which is planned and constructed to serve various purpose. Multipurpose hydropower reservoirs are designed and/or operated to provide services beyond electricity generation, such as water supply, to control the flow of water and store the water during the flood and to increase the discharge during the time of drought etc.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The useful storage is the volume of water stored in the reservoir between?

options.a: minimum pool level and maximum pool level
options.b: minimum pool level and normal pool level
options.c: normal pool level and maximum pool level
options.d: river bed and minimum pool level

solution:
A Reservoir is, most commonly, an enlarged natural or artificial lake, pond or impoundment created using a dam or lock to store water.
The maximum level to which the reservoir surface is allowed to rise during normal operating conditions is called Normal pool level. The lowest elevation to which the reservoir surface is allowed to be drawn during normal operating conditions is called the minimum pool level. The volume of water retained between these levels represents the water volume that can be used and is called active (useful) storage. The volume below the minimum pool level cannot be normally used and is called as dead storage.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The water stored in the reservoir below the minimum pool level is called?
solution:
The maximum level to which the reservoir surface is allowed to rise during normal operating conditions is called Normal pool level. The lowest elevation to which the reservoir surface is allowed to be drawn during normal operating conditions is called the minimum pool level.
The volume of water retained between these levels represents the water volume that can be used and is called active (useful) storage. The volume below the minimum pool level cannot be normally used and is called as dead storage.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: For a flood control reservoir, the effective storage is equal to?

options.c: useful storage + surcharge storage + valley storage
options.d: useful storage+ surcharge storage - valley storage.

solution:
The Maximum level to which the water surface will rise when the design flood passes over the spillway is called as Maximum pool level.
The volume of water held by the natural river channel in its valley up to the top of its banks before the construction of the reservoir is called as Valley Storage.
Surcharge storage is storage contained between maximum reservoir level and full reservoir level. It varies with the spillway capacity of a dam for given design period.
The effective storage for a flood control reservoir is
= useful storage + surcharge storage - valley storage.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Trap efficiency of a reservoir is a function of?
solution:
The reservoir sedimentation is measured in terms of its trap efficiency.
The reservoir trap efficiency is defined as the ratio of deposited sediment to the total sediment inflow for a given period within the reservoirs economic life time. Hence Trap efficiency of a reservoir is a function of the ratio of reservoir capacity to the total inflow.

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The total capacity of a reservoir is 25 million cubic meters and dead storage is 5 million cubic meters. If the average volume of sediment deposition is 0.10 million cubic meter per year, then the usefulness of the reservoir will start reducing after?
solution:
The usefulness of the reservoir will start reducing when the dead storage is completed filled with sediment deposit, after which these sediment start consuming the volume space of useful storage.
As per question data, the years after which usefulness start reducing =
$\frac{\text{Volume of dead storage}}{\text{Avg rate of sediment deposit}} = \frac{5.0}{0.1} = 50\text{ years}$.

subject: Hydrology
question_text:
The forces, which are considered for the analysis of an elementary profile of a gravity dam under empty reservoir condition, are?
(i) water pressure
(ii) self-weight
(iii) uplift
(iv) pressure due to earthquake
the correct answer is
solution:
A gravity dam is a dam constructed from concrete or stone masonry and designed to hold back water by using the self-weight of the material alone to resist the horizontal pressure of water pushing against it. Gravity dams are designed so that each section of the dam is stable and independent of any other dam section.
The elementary profile of a dam, subjected only to the external water pressure on the upstream side, will be right-angled triangle, having zero width at the water level and a base width (B) at bottom.
Only three force are considered for the analysis of an elementary profile of a gravity dam i.e.
1. self-weight
2. water pressure till top only on u/s stream side
3. Uplift Pressures. It is an upward vertical pressure created due to penetration of the water into the porous material at the dam basement.
Under empty reservoir condition, only self-weight is considered.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: In case of non-availability of space due to topography, the most suitable spillway is?
solution: A spillway is a hydraulic structure built at a dam site for diverting the surplus water from a reservoir after it has been filled to its maximum capacity. Shaft Spillway - A vertical shaft which has a funnel-shaped mouth and ends in an outlet tunnel, providing an overflow duct for a reservoir. Also known as morning glory spillway. The shaft spillway is generally used where there is non-availability of space due to topography.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: In a chute spillway, the flow is usually?
solution: Chute Spillway: It is also known as Trough Spillway. The function of Chute Spillway is to prevent damage to the valley walls that could endanger the dams. It consists of steeply sloping open channel which is made up of reinforced concrete slab. In a chute spillway the flow is usually super critical. where Froude number is greater than one ($F_r > 1$).

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: For the upstream face of an earthen dam, the most adverse condition for stability of slope is?
solution: For the upstream face of an earthen dam, the most adverse condition for stability of slope is sudden drawdown.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: If there are two canals taking off from each flank of a river, then there will be?

options.a: one divide wall and one under sluice
options.b: one divide wall and two under sluice
options.c: two divide walls and one under sluice
options.d: two divide walls and two under sluice

solution: If there are two canals taking off from each flank of a river, then there will be two divide walls and two under sluices. Under Sluices are gates-controlled openings in the weir with crest at low level. They are located on the same side as off-take canal. If two canals take off on either side of the river, it would be necessary to provide under sluices on either side.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: Generally, the weir is aligned at right angles to the direction of the main river current because?

options.a: it ensures less length of the weir

solution: A Weir or low head dam is a barrier across the width of a river that alters the flow characteristics of water and usually results in a change in the height of the river level. Generally, the weir is aligned at right angles to the direction of the main river current because: it ensures less length of the river, it gives better discharging capacity, and it is economical.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The main function of a divide wall is to?

options.a: control the silt entry in the canal
options.b: prevent river floods from entering the canal
options.c: separate the under sluices from weir proper
options.d: provide smooth flow at sufficiently low velocity

solution: The divide wall is a masonry or concrete wall constructed at right angle to the axis of the weir. The divide wall extends on the upstream side beyond the beginning of the canal head regulator; and on the downstream side, it extends up to the end of the loose protection of the under-sluices. The main functions of the divide walls: 1] It separates the 'under-sluices' with lower crest level from the 'weir proper' with higher crest level.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: A divide wall is provided?

options.a: at right angle to the axis of weir
options.b: parallel to the axis of weir and upstream of it
options.c: parallel to the axis of weir and downstream of it
options.d: at an inclination to the axis of weir

solution: The divide wall is a masonry or concrete wall constructed at right angle to the axis of the weir.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: As compared to crest of the normal portion of the weir, the crest of the under-sluice portion of weir is kept at?

options.d: any of the above depending on the design

solution: The under sluices are the openings provided at the base of the weir or barrage. These openings are provided with adjustable gates. Normally, the gates are kept closed. The crest of the under-under sluice portion of the weir is kept at a lower level (1 - 1.5 m) than the crest of the normal portion of the weir.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: Silt excluders are constructed on the?

options.a: river bed upstream of head regulator
options.b: river bed downstream of head regulator
options.c: canal bed upstream of head regulator
options.d: canal bed downstream of head regulator

solution: Silt excluder: It excludes (prevents) silt from entering the canal. It is provided on the river bed upstream of head regulator.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: If h is the ordinate of hydraulic gradient line above the top of the floor and G is specific gravity of floor material, then the thickness of floor is given by the formula?
solution: For no uplift pressure failure, thickness of floor ($t_{\min}$) = $\frac{h}{G-1}$ where 'h' is the ordinate of hydraulic gradient line above the top of the floor and 'G' is specific gravity of floor material.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: According to khosla's theory, the exit gradient in the absence of a downstream cut-off is?
solution: As per the Exit gradient by Khosla's Theory, we have Exit Gradient $G_E = \frac{1}{\pi \sqrt{\lambda}} \frac{H}{d}$. In absence of downstream cutoff, 'd' will be zero therefore, the exit gradient in the absence of a downstream cut off will be infinity.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The minimum size of stone that will remain at rest in a channel of longitudinal slope S and hydraulic mean depth R is given by?
solution: The minimum size of bed material i.e. stone that will remain at rest in a channel will be equal to d = 11RS, where R = hydraulic mean depth, S = longitudinal slope.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The ratio of average values of shear stresses produced on the bed and the banks of a channel due to flowing water is?
solution:
1. For bed of the canal average shear stress $Z_0 = Y_w RS$
2. For side slopes(banks) of the canal average shear stress $Z_0 = 0.75 Y_w RS$
so, ratio = $\frac{Y_w RS}{0.75 Y_w RS} = 1.33 > 1$ (greater than 1).

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: If the critical shear stress of a channel is $\tau_c$ then the average value of shear stress required to move grain on the bank is?
solution: The average value of shear stress on the bank = $0.75 \tau_c$, where $\tau_c$ = Critical shear stress of a channel.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: A water shed canal?

options.b: is most suitable in hilly areas
options.d: parallel to the contours of the area

solution: The canal which is aligned along any natural watershed (ridge line) is called a watershed canal, or a ridge canal. Aligning a canal (main canal or branch canal or distributary) on the ridge ensures gravity irrigation on both sides of the canal. Since the drainage flows away from the ridge, no drainage can cross a canal aligned on the ridge. Thus, a canal aligned on the watershed saves the cost of construction of cross-drainage works. A water shed canal avoids the cross drainage works.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: A canal which is aligned at right angles to the contour is called?
solution: A canal aligned nearly perpendicular to the contour of the area is called a side-slope canal. It is located neither on the ridgeline nor on the valley line but is approximately in between them. It is parallel to the natural drainage line and hence no cross-drainage works are required.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: Garret's diagram is based on?
solution: Garret diagram gives graphical method of designing the channel dimensions based on Kennedy's Theory. The diagram has discharge plotted on the abscissa. The ordinate on the left indicate the slope and on the right water depth in the channel and critical velocity $V_o$. The discharge lines are curved and the bed width lines are shown dashed.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: Garret's diagram has been drawn for?

options.b: a trapezoidal channel with side slope ½ H:1V
options.c: a trapezoidal channel with side slope 1H:1/2V

solution: Garret diagrams have been drawn for a trapezoidal channel with side slopes as 1/2:1 (1/2 horizontal: one vertical). This slope is adopted on the assumption that irrigation channels adopt approximately this shape even though they were constructed on different side slopes.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: Lacey's regime scour depth is given by?
solution: The Lacey's regime scour depth is given by $R_L = 1.35(q^2 / f)^{1/3}$ {in meter}, where, q = discharge per unit width of river, f = silt factor.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: According to Lacey's theory, the silt supporting eddies are generated from?

options.c: bottom as well as sides of channel

solution: According to Lacey's theory, the silt supporting eddies are generated from bottom as well as sides of channel. Eddies are the circular movement of water causing a small whirlpool.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: According to Lacey, the bed slope is given by?
solution: According to Lacey's theory, Bed slope(s) = $\frac{F^{5/3}}{3340Q^{1/6}}$ where F is silt factor and Q is discharge in cumec.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: As per Lacey's theory, the silt factor is?

options.a: directly proportional to average particle size
options.b: inversely proportional to average particle size
options.c: directly proportional to square root of average particle size
options.d: not related to average particle size

solution: As per Lacey's theory, the silt factor is directly proportional to square root of average particle size. Silt factor(f) = $1.76\sqrt{d}$ (where d=avg. size of silt particle, mm).

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: As per Lacey's regime theory, the flow velocity is proportional to?
solution: As per Lacey's regime theory, the flow velocity (v) = $\left(\frac{QF^2}{140}\right)^{1/6}$ m/sec.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: Wetted perimeter of a regime channel for a discharge of 64 cumec as per Lacey's theory will be?
solution: Given, discharge Q = 64 m^3/s. By using Lacey's theory, Wetted perimeter (p) = $4.75\sqrt{Q} = 4.75\sqrt{64} = 38m$.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: Which of the following canal structures is used to remove surplus water from an irrigation channel into a natural drain?
solution: Canal escape: - A canal escapes are the structure constructed on an irrigation canal for the disposal of surplus water from the canal. Structures are meant to release excess water from a canal, which could be main canal, branch canal, distributary, minors etc. It is a short of safety value.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: For a proportional outlet, the flexibility is?
solution: The outlet is said to be proportional when the rate of change of outlet discharge equals the rate of change of channel discharge. This mean Flexibility = $\frac{dq/q}{dQ/Q} = 1$.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The sensitivity of a rigid module is?
solution: Sensitivity of a module/outlet- It is defined as the ratio of rate of change of discharge through the outlet to the rate of change of the water level of the distributary. For a rigid module, the sensitivity will be equal to zero.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: Which of the following is a flexible outlet?
solution: Canal outlet is a device through which water is released from a distributing channel into a water course. Classification of Outlets: Non modular outlets, Semi modular outlets and Modular outlets. Semi-modular outlet (or flexible outlet) includes: a. Pipe outlet, b. Kennedy's gauge outlet, c. open flume outlet etc.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: A straight glacis type fall with a baffle platform and a baffle wall is called?
solution: Inglis or Baffle Fall – A straight glacis type fall with a baffle platform and a baffle wall is called Inglis fall. Here glacis is straight and sloping, but baffle wall provided on the downstream floor dissipate the energy. Main body of glacis is made of concrete. Curtain walls both at toe and heel are provided. Stone pitching are essential both at u/s and d/s ends.

subject: Water Resources Engineering
question_text: Which of the following types of falls use parabolic glacis for energy dissipation?
solution: Montague Type Canal Falls- Montage fall is similar to straight glacis fall but in this case the glacis is not straight. It is provided in parabolic shape to introduce the vertical component of velocity which improves the energy dissipation to more extent.

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The ratio of the average load to the installed capacity of the plant whose reserve capacity is zero will be equal to?
solution: The load factor is defined as the ratio of the average load over a certain period to the peak load occurring during the same period. The Utilization factor is the ratio of water actually utilized for power to that available in the river. Capacity factor or plant use factor or plant factor is the ratio of the average output of the plant to the plant capacity. Plant Factor = $\frac{\text{Average load}}{\text{Installed Capacity}}$. If the reserve capacity is zero, load factor on a particular day = capacity factor for that day. Thus, capacity factor will be identical with load factor when the maximum just equals the plant capacity. Hence option (d).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: A hydroelectric scheme operating under a head of 80 m will be classified as?
solution:
Depending upon the available head, the hydel scheme may be classified as under:
1. Low head scheme - It is the one which uses head of less than about 15 meters. It is an essential a runoff river scheme.
2. Medium head scheme - It uses head between 15 to 60 meters. Such scheme is essentially a storage scheme.
3. High head scheme - It uses a head more than 60 meters.
Given head is 80 m which is greater than 60m so, it classified as high head scheme. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: A hyetograph is a graphical representation of?
solution: The plot between rainfall intensity and time is called Hyetograph. The Hyetograph is derived from mass curve of rainfall and usually represented in a form of Bar chart. Area of hyetograph will give total amount of rainfall. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text:
Variability of rainfall is?
(i) largest in regions of high rainfall
(ii) largest in coastal areas
(iii) largest in regions of scanty rainfall

The correct answer is
solution: The degree to which rainfall amounts vary across an area or through time is an important characteristic of the climate of an area. This subject area in meteorology/climatology is called "Rainfall variability." There are two types (or components) of rainfall variability, areal and temporal. Variability of rainfall is largest in regions of scanty rainfall and least in region of high rainfall. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: In India, which of the following is adopted as standard recording rain gauge?
solution:
Rainguage is a metrological instrument used for the measurement of Rainfall data for a particular area. Rainguage are of several types:
1. Non Recording type - These type Rainguage does not directly provide rainfall data. Example is Symon's Rainguage.
2. Recording Type - These types of rainguage directly provide the rainfall data. Examples are (a) Tipping Bucket Type, (b) Weighing Bucket Type, (c) Natural Syphon type Rainguage. The Natural Syphon type rain gauge is adopted as the standard recording rain gauge in India. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The maximum average depth due to one day storm over an area of $100\text{ km}^2$ is 100mm. depth area duration (DAD) curves indicate that for the same area of $100\text{ km}^2$ the maximum average depth for a 3 hour storm will be?
solution: The development of relationship between maximum depth, area and duration for a region is known as Depth area duration curves. The curve is exponential decreasing throughout. In Depth Area Duration curve, Depth $\alpha \frac{1}{\text{area}}$ (duration of rainfall is constant). Depth is directly proportional to duration of rainfall (area=constant). Given area ($=100\text{ km}^2$) is constant. The maximum average depth for 24 hours $=100\text{mm}$. Because depth is directly proportional to duration of rainfall so, for 3-hour storm, the maximum average depth will be less than 100mm. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The maximum rainfall depth of 300mm in 24 hours has a return period of 100 years. The probability of 24 hours rainfall equal to or greater than 300mm occurring at least once in 10 year is given by?
solution: Given $n = 10$ year. A return period, also known as a recurrence interval or repeat interval, is an average time or an estimated average time between events such as earthquakes, floods, landslides, or a river discharge flows to occur. Return period ($T$) = 100 year. Probability of occurrence $P = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{100} = 0.01$. Probability of not occurrence $q = 1 - p = 1 - \frac{1}{T} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$. Probability of occurrence at least once in $n$ years hydrologic risk $(R) = 1 - q^n = 1 - (0.99)^{10}$. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The most suitable chemical which can be applied to the water surface for reducing evaporation is?
solution: Both stearyl alcohol and cetyl alcohol are used for reducing evaporation from the water body. These films act as a protective layer to prevent the evaporation from the surface. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text:
Interception losses are due to?
(i) evaporation
(ii) transpiration
(iii) stream flow

The correct answer is
solution: Interception Loss - The water that is retained by vegetation surfaces that is later evaporated into the atmosphere, or absorbed by the plants. The Interception loss prevents water from reaching the ground surface and is regarded as a primary water loss. Interception losses are due to evaporation. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: A 6-hours storm had 4 cm of rainfall and the resulting runoff was 2cm. If $\phi$ index remains at the same value, the runoff due to 10 cm of rainfall in 12 hours in the catchment is?
solution: In case 1, we have $\phi-\text{index} = \frac{4 - 2}{6} = 0.333\text{ cm/hr}$. In both cases $\phi$-index remains same. Therefore the Runoff = Total Rainfall - ($\phi$-index) * Duration of storm = $10.0\text{cm} - 0.333 \times 12\text{hr} = 10.0\text{cm} - 4.0\text{cm} = 6.0\text{cm}$. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: Which of the following methods is used to estimate flood discharge based on high water marks left over in the past?
solution: Slope area method - When it is not possible to make direct measurement especially during floods, slope-area method is a most commonly used indirect method of measurement. In this method discharge is computed on the basis of a uniform flow equation involving channel characteristics, water surface profile and a roughness coefficient. Slope area method is used to estimate flood discharge based on high water marks left over in the past. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text:
In the moving boat method of stream flow measurements, the following measurement are required:
(i) velocity and direction of current meter
(ii) speed of the boat
(iii) depths and time interval between depth readings

The correct answer is
solution:
In moving boat method, following measurements are required:
-> velocity and direction of current meter
-> depth and time interval between depth reading.
Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text:
To determine the discharge at a section in a stream from its rating curve, the required data are?
(i) slope of the water surface at the section
(ii) stage at the section
(iii) current meter reading at the section

The correct answer is
solution: The stage of a stream is defined as its water surface elevation measured above a datum. The velocity of flow at any point in the open channel can be most accurately and conveniently determined by means of a mechanical device named Current meter. In hydrology, a Rating curve is a graph of discharge versus stage for a given point on a stream. Hence, to determine the discharge from its rating curve, only stage at the section is required. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The stage of river carrying a discharge of $Q\text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$ at a point is 10 m and slope of water surface is (1/4000). The discharge of a flood at the same point at same stage of 10 m with a water surface slope of (1/1000) will be?
solution:
Given data:
Case 1: Discharge ($Q_1$) = $Q$, Slope ($S_1$) = 1/4000
Case 2: Discharge ($Q_2$) = ?, Slope ($S_2$) = 1/1000
Here in both cases same stage = 10m.
Using Manning's equation: $Q = A \times \frac{1}{n} R^{2/3} (S)^{1/2}$
$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \sqrt{\frac{S_1}{S_2}}$
Putting values in the above equation, we get:
$\frac{Q}{Q_2} = \sqrt{\frac{1/4000}{1/1000}} = \sqrt{\frac{1000}{4000}} = \sqrt{0.25} = 0.5$
$Q_2 = 2Q$. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The stream which does not have any base flow contribution is called?
solution:
Streams are classified as:
- Perennial Streams: Water flows in these streams throughout the year. The primary water source can be from ground water, surface water, or a combination of both.
- Intermittent Streams: Intermittent streams flow for part or most of the year but may carry no water during the dry season.
- Ephemeral Streams: These streams flow only for a short time, usually after a large storm or snowmelt when there is an increase in water runoff. Ephemeral streams are very small and normally have a dry channel during the year. This stream does not have any base flow contribution. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The flow-mass curve is graphical representation of?

options.b: discharge and percentage probability of flow being equalled or exceeded
options.c: cumulative discharge volume and time in chronological order
options.d: discharge and time in chronological order

solution: The flow mass curve is a plot of the cumulative discharge volume against time plotted in chronological order. The ordinate of the mass curve, $V$ at any time $t$ is thus $V = \int_{t_0}^{t} Q \cdot dt$ where $t_0$ is the time at the beginning of the curve and $Q$ is the discharge rate. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: If the demand line drawn from a ridge in a flow mass curve does not intersect the curve again, it indicates that

options.a: demand cannot be met by inflow
options.b: reservoir was not full at the beginning

solution: If the demand line drawn from a ridge in a flow mass curve does not intersect the curve again, it indicates that demand cannot be met by inflow. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The shape of recession limb of a hydrograph depends upon?
solution: Recession Limb represents the withdrawal of water from the storage built up during the early phase of hydrograph. It extends from the point of inflection at the end of the crest to the beginning of the natural groundwater flow. The shape of recession limb depends upon basin characteristics only and independent of the storm. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text:
Instantaneous unit hydrograph is a hydrograph of
(i) unit duration
(ii) unit rainfall excess
(iii) infinitely small duration
(iv) infinitely small rainfall excess

The correct answer is
solution:
The unit duration of a unit hydrograph decreases and approaches zero, such a hydrograph is called Instantaneous unit hydrograph. Instantaneous unit hydrograph is a hydrograph of:
- Unit rainfall excess.
- Infinitely small duration.
Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: For a catchment area of $120\text{ km}^2$, the equilibrium discharge in $\text{m}^3/\text{hour}$ of an S-curve obtained by the summation of 6 hour unit hydrograph is?
solution:
Given, The catchment area $= 120\text{ km}^2$, Duration of unit hydrograph $= 6$ hour.
From S-hydrograph, the equilibrium discharge $Q_{eq} = 2.778 \times \frac{A(\text{km}^2)}{D(\text{hour})} = 2.778 \times \frac{120}{6} = 55.56\text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$.
$Q_{eq} = 55.56 \times 3600 = 0.2 \times 10^6\text{ m}^3/\text{hour}$. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: A unit hydrograph has one unit of?
solution: Unit hydrograph is a direct runoff hydrograph resulting from one unit of rainfall excess (one inch or one cm) of constant intensity with uniform rainfall occurring over the entire watershed. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: The peak of a 4 hours flood hydrograph is $240\text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$. If the rainfall excess is 80 mm and base flow which is constant is $40\text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$, then the peak of 4-hour unit hydrograph will be?
solution:
We have, Peak of the flood hydrograph of 4-hour $= 240\text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$.
The flood hydrograph has a rainfall excess depth $= 80\text{mm} = 8\text{cm}$.
To get the Unit Hydrograph from the flood hydrograph of same duration, we have:
Flood Hyd. = Direct Runoff Hydrograph + Base Flow.
So, Ordinate of DRH of 4-hour = Ordinate of Flood hydrograph - ordinate of base flow.
Therefore, Peak of 4-hour DRH $= 240 - 40 = 200\text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$.
To get the ordinate of Unit Hydrograph from the DRH of same duration, divide the ordinate of DRH by the rainfall excess depth.
Therefore, Peak of the 4-hour Unit Hydrograph $= 200 / 8 = 25\text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: A 4-hour direct runoff hydrograph of a catchment is triangular in shape with a time base of 100 hours and peak flow of $50\text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$. The catchment area is $360\text{ km}^2$. The peak flow of this catchment area for a 4-hour unit hydrograph is?
solution:
The area under the Direct runoff hydrograph gives the total volume of Runoff. This area will be equal = Area of the catchment x Rainfall Excess depth(D).
Therefore, $\frac{1}{2} \times (50 \times 3600) \times 100 = 360 \times 10^6 \times D \Rightarrow D = 2.5\text{cm}$.
To get the ordinate of Unit Hydrograph from the DRH of same duration, divide the ordinate of DRH by the rainfall excess depth.
The peak ordinate of 4-hour Unit Hydrograph $= (50\text{ m}^3/\text{sec})/2.5 = 20\text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text:
To estimate the magnitude of a flood with a return period of T years, Gumbel's distribution method required the following data pertaining to annual flood series.
(i) mean value
(ii) standard deviation
(iii) length of record
(iv) coefficient of skew

The correct answer is
solution: Gumbel's distribution method is useful for obtaining values of flood discharge for a high recurrence interval. According to Gumbel, the probability of occurrence of an event equal to or larger than a value $x_0$ is given by $P(X \ge x_0) = 1 - e^{-e^{-y}}$. For estimation of flood magnitude, we require mean value, length of record, standard deviation. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology and Water Resources Engineering
question_text: For an annual flood series arranged in descending order of magnitude, the return period for magnitude listed at position period m in a total data N is?
solution: A return period, also known as a recurrence interval or repeat interval, is an average time or an estimated average time between events such as earthquakes, floods, landslides, or a river discharge flows to occur. For return period, we have according to Weibull's formula: $T = \frac{N+1}{m}$. Hence option (d).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: If the risk a flood occurring in the next 10 years is accepted to 10 % then the return period for design should be?
solution:
Given, Risk = 10% in the next 10-year
Risk = $1 - q^n$
Where 'q' represents the probability of non-occurrence.
'n' represents the duration considered.
Therefore,
$0.1 = 1 - q^n$
$q^n = 1 - 0.1 = 0.9$
$q = 0.9^{1/n} = 0.9^{1/10} = 0.9^{0.1}$
$1 - \frac{1}{T} = 0.9^{0.1}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{1}{T} = 1 - 0.9^{0.1}$
$T = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.9^{0.1})}$
where T = return period
Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Partial duration series is mostly used for?
solution: A series composed of all events during the period of record that exceed some set criterion, for example, all floods above a selected base, or all daily rainfalls greater than a specified amount. In using the partial duration series, it is necessary to establish that all events considered are independent. Hence this series is adopted mostly for rainfall analysis where the condition of independency of events are easy to establish. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: If storage, inflow rate and outflow rate are denoted by S, I and Q respectively then the value of S in Muskingum method of flood routing is given by the expression?
solution: The Muskingum method is a hydrological flow routing model with lumped parameters, which describes the transformation of discharge waves in a river bed using two equations. The first one is the continuity equation (conservation of mass) and the second equation is the relationship between the storage, inflow, and outflow of the reach (the discharge storage equation). These equations are applied within a river reach between two cross sections of a river. The Muskingum method, which was developed by McCarthy. Muskingum routing is based on an assumed linear relationship between a channel's storage and inflow and outflow discharge; and consequently, it accounts for prism and wedge storage. The storage under a line parallel to the streambed is called prism storage; the water located between this line and the actual profile is wedge storage. The routing parameters in the models are usually derived by calibration using measured discharge hydrographs. In the Muskingum method the storage S in the routing reach is represented by the following discharge-storage equation: $S = K [X I + (1 - X) Q]$ where K and X are coefficients. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology
question_text:
Which of the following are saint Venant's equations for unsteady open-channel flow?
(i) continuity equation
(ii) momentum equation
(iii) energy equation
solution:
The Saint Venant's Equations were formulated in the 19th century by two mathematicians, de Saint Venant and Bousinnesque. The solution of the St. Venant equations is known as dynamic routing, which is generally the standard to which other methods are measured or compared.
1) Continuity equation:
2) $\frac{dQ}{dx} + \frac{dA}{dt} = 0$
3) Momentum Equation:
$\frac{1}{A} \frac{dQ}{dt} + \frac{1}{A} \frac{d}{dx} (\frac{Q^2}{A}) + g \frac{dy}{dx} - g(S_0 - S_f) = 0$
Where Q-Discharge through the channel, A-Area of cross-section of flow, y-Depth of flow, $S_0$-Channel bottom slope, $S_f$-Friction slope. The above differentials are partial differential equation. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: In case of channel routing, the storage is a function of?
solution: The Muskingum method is a hydrological flow routing model with lumped parameters, which describes the transformation of discharge waves in a river bed using two equations. The first one is the continuity equation (conservation of mass) and the second equation is the relationship between the storage, inflow, and outflow of the reach (the discharge storage equation). Muskingum routing is based on an assumed linear relationship between a channel's storage and inflow and outflow discharge; and consequently, it accounts for prism and wedge storage. In the Muskingum method the storage S in the routing reach is represented by the following discharge-storage equation: $S = K [X I + (1 - X) Q]$ Where 'I' represents the inflow discharge and 'Q' represents the outflow discharge. Therefore, in channel routing, the storage is a function of both inflow and outflow discharge. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Muskingum method of flood routing is?
solution: The Muskingum method is a hydrological flow channel routing model with lumped parameters, which describes the transformation of discharge waves in a river bed using two equations. The first one is the continuity equation (conservation of mass) and the second equation is the relationship between the storage, inflow, and outflow of the reach (the discharge storage equation). These equations are applied within a river reach between two cross sections of a river. The Muskingum method, which was developed by McCarthy. Muskingum routing is based on an assumed linear relationship between a channel's storage and inflow and outflow discharge; and consequently, it accounts for prism and wedge storage. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology
question_text:
Which of the following equations are used in hydrological flood routing methods?
(i) continuity equation
(ii) equation of motion
(iii) energy equation
solution: Hydrologic routing uses the continuity equation for hydrology. In its simplest form, inflow to the river reach is equal to the outflow of the river reach plus the change of storage. $I = O + \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta t}$ where 'I' is average inflow to the reach during $\Delta t$, 'O' is average outflow from the reach during $\Delta t$, 'S' is the water currently in the reach (known as storage). Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: In linear reservoir, storage varies linearly with?
solution: A linear reservoir is defined as in which the storage(S) and outflow(O), are related by the equation, $S = KO$ in which the proportionality factor K is a reservoir detention characteristic referred to as a storage coefficient. Therefore, in linear reservoir storage varies linearly with outflow rate. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The dimensions of storage coefficient are?
solution: Storativity or the storage coefficient is the volume of water released from storage per unit decline in hydraulic head in the aquifer, per unit area of the aquifer. Storativity is a dimensionless quantity, and is always greater than 0. Hence option (d).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The porosity of a sand simple is 50%, The specific yield of an aquifer containing this sand will be?
solution: Specific yield is defined as the volume of water released from storage by an unconfined aquifer per unit surface area of aquifer per unit decline of the water table. $n = S_y + S_r$ where $n$ is total porosity [dimensionless], $S_y$ is specific yield [dimensionless] and $S_r$ is specific retention [dimensionless], the amount of water retained by capillary forces during gravity drainage of an aquifer. Thus, specific yield, which is sometimes called effective porosity. Given, $n = 50\% = 0.5$. So, specific yield will be less than 50%. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Discharge per unit drawdown at a well is called?
solution: Specific capacity is a quantity that which a water well can produce per unit of drawdown. It is normally obtained from a step drawdown test. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Permeability of a soil sample at a temperature of $20^\circ\text{C}$ is 0.5mm/sec. If the temperature is decreases to $15^\circ\text{C}$, the permeability of the same soil sample is?
solution: Soil permeability is the property of the soil to transmit water and air and is one of the most important qualities to consider for fish culture. As the viscosity of the pore fluid decrease with the temperature, permeability increases with temperature, as unit weight of pore fluid does not change much with change in temperature. Hence the permeability of soil at $15^\circ\text{C}$ will be less than that at $20^\circ\text{C}$. Therefore, it will be less than 0.5 mm/sec. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Due to decreases in atmospheric pressure, the water level in a well penetrating a confined aquifer will?
solution: An Aquifer is an underground layer of water-bearing permeable rock, rock fractures or unconsolidated materials (gravel, sand, or silt). A confined aquifer is an aquifer below the land surface that is saturated with water. Layers of impermeable material are both above and below the aquifer, causing it to be under pressure so that when the aquifer is penetrated by a well, the water will rise above the top of the aquifer. A water-table or unconfined aquifer is an aquifer whose upper water surface (water table) is at atmospheric pressure, and thus is able to rise and fall. Water-table aquifers are usually closer to the Earth's surface than confined aquifers are, and as such are impacted by drought conditions sooner than confined aquifers. Due to decrease in atmospheric pressure the water level in a well penetrating a confined aquifer will increases. Hence option (b).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: According to Indian standards, the number of rain gauge stations for area of $5200\text{ km}^2$ in plains should be?
solution: In plain area-1 station per $520\text{ km}^2$ as per the Indian standard. So, for area of $5200\text{ km}^2$ required number of rain gauge will be equal to 10. Hence option (a).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: For a storm of given duration, if the intensity of storm corresponding to a return period of T is I, then the intensity of storm for same duration but corresponding to a return period of $T_1$ (where $T_1 > T$) will be?
solution: Rainfall at a place can be completely define if the intensity, duration and frequency of various storms occurring at a place are completely known. The frequency data of storm of various duration, so obtained can be represented by Intensity Duration Frequency curve (IDF). The IDF curve is a graph between average rainfall intensity in cm/hour and duration in minutes, whose equation is given below. $P = \frac{A T^x}{(t_r + B)^n}$ Where 'P' represents the rainfall intensity in cm/hour. 'T' represents the return period. 't_r' represents the duration of storm. A, B, n, x, represents the constant. Therefore, using the above equation, If return period increases than the intensity of storm also increases, keeping the storm duration constant. Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: What is the probability of an equal to or greater than 25 years flood occurring twice in the next three years?
solution:
Probability of occurrence of rainfall $\gamma$ times in $\eta$ years
$P = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{25}$
$q = 1 - p = 1 - \frac{1}{25} = \frac{24}{25}$
Here $\eta = 3$, $\gamma = 2$
$P_{\gamma \eta} = \frac{\eta!}{(\eta - \gamma)! \gamma!} p^\gamma q^{(\eta - \gamma)}$
$= \frac{3!}{(3 - 2)! 2!} (\frac{1}{25})^2 (\frac{24}{25})^{3-2}$
$= \frac{3 \times 2}{2 \times 25 \times 25} \times \frac{24}{25} = \frac{72}{15625}$
Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The rainfall in four successive 12 hours period on a catchment are 40, 80, 90 and 30 mm. If the infiltration index $\phi$ for the storm is 5mm/hours, then total surface runoff will be?
solution:
The rainfall in four successive 12 hours period on a catchment are 40, 80, 90 and 30mm because each period is for 12 hours.
($\phi$-index) $\times 12 = 5\text{mm/sec} \times 12 = 60\text{mm}$
whose rainfall value is less than 60mm those value will not be count in total rainfall.
I. = 40mm (for 12 hour)
II. = 80mm (for 12 hour)
III. = 90mm (for 12 hour)
IV. = 30mm (for 12 hour)
So, total rainfall = 80 + 90 = 170mm
effective time period = 12 + 12 = 24hour
Total rainfall = ($\phi$-index) $\times$ time period + Runoff
$170 = 5 \times (12 + 12) + \text{Runoff}$
$\text{Runoff} = 170 - 120 = 50\text{mm}$.
Hhece option (b).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: The 6 hour unit hydrograph of catchment of area $180\text{ km}^2$ is triangular in shape, If the peak ordinate of this hydrograph is $10\text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$, then the time base is?
solution:
Given, Area = $180\text{ km}^2$
$Q_{pk} = 10\text{ m}^3/\text{sec}$.
For 6-hour unit hydrograph, we have rainfall excess depth equal to 1 cm.
Area under the hydrograph gives the volume of total runoff. And this volume should be equal to multiplication of catchment area and rainfall excess depth. Therefore, we have
Area of triangle = $1/2 \times (base) \times (height)$
$\frac{1}{2} \times t_b \times 3600 \times Q_{pk} = A_c \times 10^{-2}$
$\frac{1}{2} \times t_b \times 3600 \times 10 = 180 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-2}$
$t_b = 100\text{hours}$
Hence option (c).

subject: Hydrology
question_text: Percentage of rain gauge station which should be equipped with self-recording gauges for knowing the intensity of rainfall should be about?
